alsoKnownAs: Gulf Coast Waterdog (Necturus beyeri, sister species sold under the same trade name)

nativeRange.habitat: Cool, well-oxygenated permanent waters across eastern North America — lakes, rivers, large streams, and reservoirs with rocky or debris-strewn bottoms that provide daytime cover. Most commonly observed at depths of roughly 0.2–1.0 m but has been recorded as deep as ~30 m.

decorPreferences: Multiple large flat rocks, slate caves, or ceramic flowerpots arranged as overhangs and hides — every animal needs its own territory
Fine sand or smooth large pebbles only (avoid medium gravel — impaction risk during suction feeding)
Driftwood for cover (hardwoods only; avoid pine and cedar, which release salamander-toxic oils)
Dim or shaded lighting — strongly light-averse; floating plants or a partial tank cover help
Powerful but baffled filtration with strong biological capacity; mudpuppies are messy carnivores and water-quality crashes are commonly fatal
Tightly-fitted screened or weighted lid — has been reported to push through unsecured tops
Active chilling (chiller or basement room) generally required; tanks above 25°C have been reported as fatal within days

diet.notes: Strict carnivore. Has been documented eating earthworms and nightcrawlers (the captive staple), small fish such as minnows or shiners, crayfish, aquatic insects and their larvae, snails and freshwater clams, leeches, annelids, and occasionally small amphibians. A typical adult feeding is two or three large earthworms or minnows offered two or three times per week; remove uneaten prey promptly. Has been reported to tolerate long fasting periods, so refusal of food for several weeks after acquisition (especially in wild-caught animals) is not unusual.

goodWith: Conspecifics of similar adult size only when given separate territories, abundant cover, and a much larger tank than the single-animal minimum
avoidWith: Any fish small enough to be swallowed
Small ornamental shrimp and snails (treated as food)
Tropical fish or any tankmate requiring warmer water (incompatible temperature)
Other Necturus species or other large aquatic salamanders (inter-amphibian aggression and disease-mixing risk)
Non-quarantined frogs, newts, or other amphibians (chytrid and Bsal transmission risk)
Fin-nippy fish (external gills are easily damaged and slow to heal)

summary: The Common Mudpuppy is a fully aquatic North American salamander that retains its feathery external gills for life through obligate paedomorphosis, meaning it never transforms into a land form. Adults reach 20 to 40 cm and require a chilled, dimly lit aquarium of at least 280 litres.
careNotes: The Common Mudpuppy is a fully aquatic salamander that retains its bushy external gills and never metamorphoses — a permanent larval state known as paedomorphosis. It is one of the very few truly COLDWATER amphibians kept in the hobby: water above 25°C has been reported as potentially fatal, and most husbandry guidance advises keeping the tank below 20°C year-round (a long-term band of 5–22°C is broadly accepted, with winter cooling to around 10–15°C tolerated and even beneficial for seasonal conditioning). An aquarium chiller, basement room, or unheated outbuilding is generally required outside of cold climates; this single constraint is what makes the species unsuitable for most typical home setups. Substrate should be fine sand or smooth large pebbles only — like axolotls and sirens, mudpuppies feed by suction and have been reported to ingest gravel, with fatal gastrointestinal impaction documented. Lighting must be dim: bright tanks tend to visibly stress the species. Permeable amphibian skin and bushy gills mean copper-based medications, untreated tap water, soap residues on hands and equipment, and most fish medications should be avoided. Almost the entire pet and research trade is supplied by wild-caught animals — captive breeding is essentially undocumented in the hobby, with one published success requiring a very large tank with continuous current and a 6–16°C temperature cycle. The closely related Gulf Coast Waterdog (Necturus beyeri) is occasionally sold under the same trade names and shares essentially identical care requirements at a slightly smaller adult size of around 15–22 cm; the two are sometimes sold interchangeably. The species is susceptible to chytrid fungi (Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis and B. salamandrivorans), so a quarantine period of at least 30 days for any new arrival and strict isolation from other amphibians are strongly advised. Several US states list the species as threatened, vulnerable, or endangered — check local regulations before acquisition.
breedingNotes: Breeding in the wild has been reported to begin with autumn courtship and spring egg deposition (late April to early June). Females attach 60-100 eggs (occasionally up to ~200) to the underside of submerged rocks or logs and guard them until hatching after roughly 40-60 days. Captive breeding is essentially absent from the hobby; one published success used a large (1.7 × 0.8 m) tank with continuous current and a 6-16°C temperature cycle. Captive reproduction should not be considered a realistic goal for home keepers.

primary.name: Caudata Culture — Necturus maculosus
primary.notes: Primary source for the coldwater requirement (below 20°C preferred; over 25°C may prove fatal), tank dimensions (~75 cm minimum for one adult, 75 gal+ for multiples), substrate guidance, diet (3 large minnows or nightcrawlers 2-3×/week), territorial behaviour, sole-source confirmation that virtually all trade animals are wild-caught, and adult length up to 49 cm.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Common mudpuppy
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Proteidae, Urodela), distribution (eastern US and southeastern Canada), depth range (shallows to ~30 m), adult length (avg 33 cm, max 43.5 cm), wild lifespan (~11 years), obligatory paedomorphosis, carnivorous diet, and clutch size (20-200, average 60).

additional.name: That Reptile Blog — Natural History and Captive Care of the Mudpuppy (Frank Indiviglio)
additional.notes: Tertiary source for captive lifespan (commonly 15-20+ years, individuals reported beyond 25 years), filtration/light-aversion notes, and reinforcement of the wild-caught-only supply chain. AmphibiaWeb (the originally-listed tertiary) returned a CAPTCHA wall, so this source was swapped in to meet the 2-source minimum-confirmation rule for lifespan and tank size.

additional.name: Salamantra — Gulf Coast Waterdogs (Necturus beyeri)
additional.notes: Reference for the sister species (Necturus beyeri) sold under the same trade names — confirms similar care needs, sand-burrowing behaviour, escape risk, and the rule against mixing Necturus species in one tank.

amphibian.breedingNotes: Wild breeding has been reported to begin in autumn with courtship, followed by spring egg deposition typically between late April and early June. Females attach 60 to 100 eggs to the undersides of submerged rocks or logs and guard them until hatching, which takes roughly 40 to 60 days. Captive breeding is essentially undocumented in the hobby; one published account required a large tank with continuous current and a temperature cycle of 6 to 16°C. Breeding in a home aquarium setting is not a realistic goal.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Strict coldwater requirement (10-20°C; over 25°C is commonly fatal) demands a chiller or unheated room year-round. Adults reach 20-40 cm and need a 280 L+ tightly-sealed tank with dim lighting and individual hide territories. The hobby supply is essentially all wild-caught with some US states listing the species as threatened or endangered. Inappropriate for typical home aquarists; suited to dedicated specialist keepers prepared for a chilled, species-only, multi-decade setup.
